content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 6, 1993


Robert J. Lanzone
Town Attorney
Town of Woodside
2955 Woodside Road
P.O. Box 620005
Woodside, CA 94062

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-135

Dear Mr. Lanzone:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Town Councilmember Susan Crocker regarding her
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Woodside Town Councilmember Crocker participate in the
consideration of a specific plan for the Woodside Glens area of
the town if she owns a residence within the area?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Crocker may not participate in the
consideration of the specific plan if any aspect of the plan will
have a material financial effect on her real property as discussed
below.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-135
Page 2


However, specific decisions on the various components of the
specific plan may be considered separately. Thus, once decisions
have been reached on the portions of the specific plan for which
the councilmember has a disqualifying financial interest, the
councilmember may participate in subsequent deliberations so long
as: (1) those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in
any way affect the decision from which the councilmember was
disqualified, and (2) those decisions will not have a material
financial effect on any of the councilmember's economic interests.

FACTS

The Woodside Town Council has been involved in specific plan
decisions concerning the Woodside Glens Specific Plan Area. The
specific plan area is 80 acres of land in the city. Fourteen to
fifteen percent of the population resides in the area. The Town
of Woodside is 14.5 square miles with a population of 5,000
persons.

According to your facts, the boundaries of the specific plan
area have already been set. At this time, the town council will
be involved in determining specific issues related to the specific
plan area, such as the treatment of existing open spaces in the
plan area, creation of emergency access routes, the widening and
mending of roads, limiting street parking, and improvement of
sewer and water service within the area.

Councilmember Crocker owns a single-family residence in the
Woodside Glens Specific Plan Area.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using the official's
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. An official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the
official's immediate family, or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Section 87103(b).

Councilmember Crocker owns a single-family residence which is
presumably worth over $1,000 in the Woodside Glens Specific Plan
Area. Therefore, she may not participate in any decision
regarding the Woodside Glens Specific Plan if it is reasonably

Our File No. A-93-135
Page 3


foreseeable² that the decision will have a material financial
effect on her real property. (Section 87103.)

The standard for materiality differs depending on whether the
property is directly or indirectly involved in a decision. Where
the decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision of the councilmember's property the
issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land
use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of her
property; the imposition or repeal or modification of any taxes or
fees assessed or imposed on her property; or redevelopment of the
councilmember's property, the councilmember's real property is
considered directly involved and the councilmember may not
participate. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).)

Additionally, Regulation 18702.3 sets forth standards for
determining materiality with respect to governmental decisions
which indirectly affect real property. Among other things, the
indirect effect of the decision on an official's real property is
material if:

     (1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

     (2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

     (3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or

2 Whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Commission (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-93-135
Page 4


the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

     (A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or

     (B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.

                           Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).

According to your facts, the boundaries of the specific plan
area have already been set. At this time, the town council will
be involved in determining specific issues related to the specific
plan area, such as the treatment of existing open spaces in the
plan area, creation of emergency access routes, the widening and
mending of roads, limiting street parking, and improvement of
sewer and water service within the area. It would appear that
each of these decisions will have some financial effect on the
councilmember's real property. We cannot determine whether these
effects will be material. We must leave this determination to you
and the town councilmember within the guidelines set forth above.

"Public Generally" Exception

     The remaining issue is whether the specific plan decisions
for the Woodside Glens area will affect the councilmember in a
manner that is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. For the "public generally" exception to apply, a
decision must affect the councilmember's interests in
substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant
segment of the population of the Town of Woodside. (Regulation
18703; Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214.)

     You stated that the specific plan area is 80 acres of land in
the city, and that 14 to 15 percent of the population resides in
the area. Thus, it would appear that those decisions that will
affect the entire specific plan area in substantially the same
manner as they will affect the councilmember's property would fall
within the "public generally" exception.

     However, as we discussed in our telephone conversation of
April 12, 1993, not all aspects of the plan will affect the entire
area in a similar manner. For example, some aspects of the plan
could affect streets immediately adjacent to the councilmember's
property. These decisions could significantly affect the value of
the councilmember's property while not affecting all the other

Our File No. A-93-135
Page 5


properties in the plan area. Thus, final adoption of the plan
would not fall within the "public generally" exception under your
facts.3

We have, however, previously advised that large and complex
decisions may, under certain circumstances, be divided into
separate decisions4 so that an official who has a disqualifying
interest in one component of the decision may still participate as
to other components in which the official has no financial
interest. (Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; Huffaker Advice
Letter, No. A-86-343.)

Thus, if the specific plan is segmented so that the various
decisions are considered separately, the councilmember may be able
to participate in many of the decisions leading up to the adoption
of the specific plan. The following procedure may be used to
permit the councilmember to participate in some of the decisions:

1. The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from the other
decisions;

2. The decisions for which the councilmember is disqualified
must be considered first, and a final decision reached without her
participation;

3. Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
specific plan for which the councilmember has a disqualifying
interest, the councilmember may participate in subsequent
deliberations regarding other portions of the specific plan, so
long as: (1) those deliberations do not result in a reopening or
in any way affect the decision from which the councilmember was
disqualified, and (2) those decisions will not have a material
financial effect on any of the councilmember's economic interests.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)


3 The regulations to the Act provide an additional exception for
small jurisdictions. Pursuant to Regulation 18703.1,
jurisdictions of 25,000 persons or less covering a geographic area
of ten miles or less are afforded a special "public generally"
exception. However, the Town of Woodside contains 14.5 square
miles of land.

4 Under some circumstances, a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be segmented. (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-
119.) For example, if a decision concerning one portion of the
specific plan could decide or alter a decision for which the
councilmember has a conflict of interest, the official would be
disqualified as to both decisions. (Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-
038.) Further, where two decisions are alternatives, the
decisions are too interlinked to be considered separately.

Our File No. A-93-135
Page 6


However, as stated above, the councilmember would not be able
to vote on the final adoption of the plan since aspects of the
plan may materially affect her real property interests in unusual
ways.

We note that in our advice letter to Michael Estrada (No. A-
92-501), we advised that an official with a conflict of interest
with respect to components of a plan may participate in the
final adoption of the plan. We have applied such an exception
to general plans because a general plan, by definition, will
affect the public as a whole. (See, Marino Advice Letter, No. I-
89-291 regarding the adoption of a general plan.)

However, this exception does not apply to specific plans.
Specific plans may affect a small portion of the jurisdiction.
Thus, absent facts to indicate that the affected area is a
significant segment of the jurisdiction, and that the plan would
affect the segment in substantially the same manner, the "public
generally" exception discussed in the Marino Advice Letter would
not be applicable.5 We do not have sufficient facts at this time
to determine if this is the case.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


5 To the extent that the Estrada Advice Letter conflicts with
this conclusion, it is superseded.

6 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

the town if she owns a residence within the area?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Crocker may not participate in the
consideration of the specific plan if any aspect of the plan will

Division
